title: Everyday Slang
description: The casual French young locals actually use - greetings, reactions, and filler you will not find in a textbook.
situation: Hanging out with friends: reacting to news, making plans, and sounding natural instead of stiff.

phrases.tip: The default casual greeting and goodbye with friends. Use 'Bonjour' with strangers or in shops.
Literally 'it goes?'. The reply is usually 'ça va' right back. The all-purpose check-in.
Literally 'what's new?'. A friendly opener between people who know each other.
Literally 'too good'. 'Trop' is the go-to youth intensifier for 'really' or 'so'.
'Ouf' is verlan (back-slang) for 'fou' (crazy). Very current among younger speakers.

phrases.english: I love it / I'm into it
phrases.tip: From Arabic 'kif'. 'Je kiffe ce son' = I love this track. Hugely common in casual speech.
'Chelou' is verlan for 'louche' (shady). For anything that feels off or suspicious.
The universal filler word when you can't name something. 'Passe-moi le truc' = pass me the whatsit.
Literally 'that walks'. The standard way to agree to a plan. Widely acceptable everywhere.

phrases.english: Let's catch up / I'll hit you up
phrases.tip: Casual way to say you'll be in touch to meet up. Common in texts among friends.
Short for 'dégouté' (disgusted). For disappointment, not literal disgust. 'Trop dég' = so bummed.
Literally 'punctured', like a flat tyre. The everyday way to say you're shattered.
Literally 'mowed down'. The standard casual way to say you have no money.
Literally 'we go there'. The natural rallying call to head out. Fine in any casual setting.
Dropped into speech as filler the same way English uses 'like'. 'Genre, j'sais pas' = like, I dunno.
Used alone as emphatic agreement. Someone says something true, you reply 'grave' = 'so true'.
